Dr Bill Ellis Partial Differentiation School of Mathematics and Statistics The University of New South Wales Sydney, Australia The Chain Rule(s) Email: wjellis@unsw.edu.au

Taylor Polynomials and Error Estimates

Leibniz’s Rule

January 19, 2022 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Plots of Functions of Several Variables The plane ax + by + cz = d z I In Engineering and Science, we often need to deal with complicated systems that depend on many variables. For example, the ideal gas equation relates the pressure P of d a gas in a container as a function of the temperature T of c

the gas and the volume V of the container: T P (T, V ) = k , (k is a constant). d V 0 b y I Recall that the graph of a function of one variable d y = f (x) is a curve in two-dimensional space R2 . The a graph of a function of two variables z = f (x, y) is a surface in three-dimensional space R3 . x I The co-ordinate axes x, y, z form a right-handed system.

Summary Partial Differentiation I The co-ordinate axes x, y, z form a right-handed system. I If y = f (x) where f : R → R, then y is the dependent variable, and x is the independent variable. The I ax + by + cz = d is a plane. derivative, dy/dx or f ′ (x), is the rate of change of y with respect to x. I (x − x0 )2 + (y − y0 )2 + (z − z0 )2 = a2 is a sphere centred I If z = f (x, y) where f : R2 → R, then z is the dependent at (x0 , y0 , z0 ) with radius a. variable, and x and y are the independent variables. I z = c(x2 + y 2 ) is a paraboloid of revolution. I If we treat y as a constant and differentiate with respect p to x we obtain ∂f /∂x, the partial derivative of f with I z = c x2 + y 2 is a cone with semi-vertical respect to x. This number is the rate of change of z in angle tan−1 (1/c). the x direction (holding y fixed). I If we treat x as a constant and differentiate with respect I If a variable is missing in the equation, then the function to y we obtain ∂f /∂y, the partial derivative of f with is independent of this variable. Extend the two respect to y. This number is the rate of change of z in dimensional curve to infinity in this direction. the y direction (holding x fixed). . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Higher order derivatives We also denote the second derivatives using subscripts: ∂f ∂f Assume that the partial derivatives and of f : R2 → R ∂ 2f ∂ 2f ∂x ∂y fxx = , fxy = , exist for all (x, y). ∂x2 ∂y∂x ∂ 2f ∂ 2f fyx = , fyy = . ∂f ∂f ∂x∂y ∂y 2 Then and are also functions of the independent ∂x ∂y variables x and y, and we can differentiate again to obtain Note the order of x and y in the mixed derivatives. four second-order partial derivatives     It can happen that fxy ̸= fyx . However, these mixed ∂ 2f ∂ ∂f ∂ 2f ∂ ∂f := , := , derivatives are guaranteed to be equal if all the first- and ∂x2 ∂x ∂x ∂y ∂x ∂y ∂x second-order partial derivatives are continuous. You can     ∂ 2f ∂ ∂f ∂ 2f ∂ ∂f assume this condition for all functions you will see in this := , := . ∂x ∂y ∂x ∂y ∂y 2 ∂y ∂y course, unless otherwise stated.

Example 13 The Chain Rule(s) Let z = f (x, y) = x2 sin y + x3 y + y 5 . Find I A common situation is that z is a function of x and y, 2 2 2 2 ∂ z ∂ z ∂ z ∂ z with x and y themselves functions of other variables, , , , . ∂x2 ∂y 2 ∂x∂y ∂y∂x say u and v. Then z is also a function of u and v, and it ∂z makes sense to ask the question “What is ?” ∂u I The chain rule enables us to answer this question without actually ever having to calculate z as an explicit function of u and v. It is usually simpler to use the chain rule than to differentiate directly the compositive function

w(u, v) := z(x(u, v), y(u, v)).

I Three versions of the chain rule are shown below. However, all you need to remember is that you just apply the single-variable chain rule to each argument of z. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .  Revision: single-variable chain rule Chain rule I If z = f (x) and x = x(t), then dz dz dx If z = f (x, y), x = x(t) and y = y(t), then = . dt dx dt dz ∂z dx ∂z dy = + . dt ∂x dt ∂y dt

dx x ∂z dx dz dt ∂x dt dx t x z t z dy ∂z y dt ∂y

Example 20 Taylor Polynomials and Error Estimates Let w = f (u, v) with u = x + y and v = x − y. Show that I It can sometimes be convenient to approximate a  2  2 complicated function by a polynomial. ∂w ∂w ∂w ∂w = − . ∂x ∂y ∂u ∂v I For example, when |x| is small,

x3 sin x ≈ x − , 3! x2 x4 cos x ≈ 1 − + , 2! 4! x x3 x4 ex ≈ 1 + x + + + , 2! 3! 4! x2 x3 x4 log(1 + x) ≈ x − + − . 2 3 4 I In each of the above examples, the RHS is a Taylor polynomial for the LHS, expanded about x = 0. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Example 22 Definition 21 Let f (x) = ex . The Taylor polynomial of degree n for a function f (x) about a 1. Find the Taylor polynomial Pn (x) for f about the is point a = 0. X n f (k) (a) 2. For n = 1, 2, 3, sketch the graph of Pn on the Pn (x) = (x − a)k k! interval [−1, 1]. k=0

′ f (n) (a) = f (a) + f (a)(x − a) + · · · + (x − a)n . n!

I It can be shown that f (x) ≈ Pn (x) when x is close to a. The higher the degree n, the better the approximation. I The infinite sum obtained by sending n → ∞ is called the Taylor series of f (x) about a.

. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .   Functions of two variables 5 Definition 23 The Taylor polynomial of degree n for f (x, y) about (a, b) is

Pn (x, y) X k   1 X k n ∂kf = (a, b) (x − a)k−j (y − b)j k=0 k! j=0 j ∂xk−j ∂y j

X n X k ∂kf (x − a)k−j (y − b)j 0 = (a, b) . P1(x) k=0 j=0 ∂xk−j ∂y j (k − j)! j! P2(x) −1 P3(x) ex −2 −4 −2 0 2 4 It can be shown that f (x, y) ≈ Pn (x, y) when (x, y) is close to (a, b). The higher the degree n, the better the approximation. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

I Since the formulae above are rather complicated, in this Notes course we consider only Taylor polynomials of degree one I The graph of z = P1 (x, y) is the tangent plane at (a, b) and two. to the surface z = f (x, y); see Topic 2. I The Taylor polynomial of degree one for f (x, y) about (a, b) is I The function P1 (x, y) is a linear combination of 1, x and y. Hence, P1 (x, y) is a polynomial of degree one in two P1 (x, y) = f (a, b) + fx (a, b) (x − a) + fy (a, b) (y − b). variables x and y. I The Taylor polynomial of degree two is I The function P2 (x, y) is a linear combination of 1, x, y, x2 , xy and y 2 . Therefore, P2 (x, y) is a polynomial of P2 (x, y) = f (a, b) + fx (a, b) (x − a) + fy (a, b) (y − b) degree two in x and y. 1 + fxx (a, b) (x − a)2 2 I In this course, the question “Find the Taylor series + 2fxy (a, b) (x − a)(y − b) of f (x, y) = · · · about the point (a, b) = · · · up to and  including quadratic terms” means “Find the Taylor + fyy (a, b) (y − b) . polynomial P2 (x, y) of f (x, y) = · · · about the point (a, b) = · · · ”. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .  Matching derivatives property Example 25 Find the Taylor series of f (x, y) = x4 y 3 about the point (1, 2) up to and including quadratic terms.

Theorem 24 The Taylor polynomial Pn (x, y) satisfies

∂ k Pn ∂kf (a, b) = (a, b) for i + j = k ≤ n, ∂xi ∂y j ∂xi ∂y j but ∂ k Pn (a, b) = 0 for i + j = k > n. ∂xi ∂y j

. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Example 26 Calculate the Taylor polynomial of degree at most 2 of f (x, y) = ex cos y about (a, b) = (0, 0). Compare the value of f (−0.1, 0.2) with its Taylor approximation.

P2 (x, y) = 8+32(x−1)+12(y−2)+48(x−1)2 +48(x−1)(y−2)+6(y−2)2 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .   Estimating errors I Recall the linear approximation f (x, y) ≈ P1 (x, y) about (a, b) obtained from the first three terms of the Taylor series:

f (x, y) ≈ f (a, b) + fx (a, b)(x − a) + fy (a, b)(y − b).

I Letting h = x − a and k = y − b, we can write the linear approximation as

f (a + h, b + k) ≈ f (a, b) + fx (a, b) h + fy (a, b) k

for (h, k) near (0, 0). 1 I Equivalently, for small changes ∆x and ∆y in the values P2 (x, y) = 1 + x + (x2 − y 2 ), of x and y, we have f (−0.1, 0.2) = 0.88680091, P2 (−0.1, 0.2) = 0.885 f (x + ∆x, y + ∆y) ≈ f (x, y) + fx (x, y) ∆x + fy (x, y) ∆y. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

I Hence, Example 27 Suppose the temperature T is given by T (x, y) = x2 + y 3 . ∂f ∂f Find the approximate error in T if x = 1, y = 2, ∆x = 0.1, f (x + ∆x, y + ∆y) − f (x, y) ≈ ∆x + ∆y. ∂x ∂y and ∆y = −0.3. I Let ∆f = f (x + ∆x, y + ∆y) − f (x, y) denote the change in the value of f arising from the (small) changes ∆x and ∆y in the values of x and y. The linear approximation can then be written as ∂f ∂f ∆f ≈ ∆x + ∆y. ∂x ∂y I For example, ∆x and ∆y might be small errors in some measured values of x and y. The formula lets us estimate the corresponding error ∆f when we calculate f (x, y). −3.4 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .  Maximum possible error Example 28 Suppose the temperature T is given by T (x, y) = x2 + y 3 . I In Example 27, we knew the exact value and sign of the Find the maximum possible errors ∆x and ∆y, but usually all we know is the range of 1. absolute error, possible errors. For example, if we know that our 2. relative error, and independent variables can take values x ± ∆x 3. percentage error and y ± ∆y, then the maximum possible errors in x and y in T if x = 1, y = 2, |∆x| ≤ 0.1, and |∆y| ≤ 0.3. are |∆x| and |∆y|, respectively. The maximum possible error in the function f (x, y) can then be estimated by

∂f ∂f |∆f | . |∆x| + |∆y|. ∂x ∂y

I Notice that the absolute value symbols mean we always add together non-negative numbers!

Example 29 The volume V of a cone with radius r and perpendicular height h is given by V = 13 πr2 h. Determine the maximum absolute error and the maximum percentage error in calculating V given that r = 5cm and h = 3cm to the nearest millimetre.

3.8, 3.8 ≈ 0.42, 42% . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .   2.87 2.87, 78.54 × 100 ≈ 3.7% . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Relative changes Example 30 Show that if f (x, y) = Cxα y β where C, α and β are constants, with C > 0, then Suppose f (x, y) > 0 and let g(x, y) = log f (x, y).
